title: Why has my energy direct debit gone up?
description: Seven reasons your energy direct debit has gone up, from price cap changes to estimated readings, plus how to check the maths and challenge an unfair rise.
blurb: The seven reasons suppliers raise monthly payments, how to check the maths yourself, and how to challenge an unfair increase.
lede: An email lands saying your monthly energy payment is changing, and the new number is higher. Sometimes a lot higher, even though you have not changed how you live and your account might even be in credit. It feels arbitrary, but it never is: a direct debit increase always traces back to one of a handful of causes, and every one of them can be checked with five minutes and a calculator. This guide walks through how suppliers set the monthly figure, the seven reasons it goes up, how to verify the new amount against your own usage, and what to do if the sums do not add up.
short_answer: Your direct debit is your supplier's estimate of your annual energy cost divided into twelve equal payments, so it rises when unit rates go up, when your usage estimate increases, or when your account balance is lower than expected. Under the July to September 2026 Ofgem cap, a typical dual-fuel household pays roughly £1,863 a year, about £155 a month. If your new payment implies an annual cost well above your actual usage at current rates, you can ask the supplier to justify it and they must either explain or adjust it.

sections.h2: How your direct debit is actually calculated
sections.html:
A fixed monthly direct debit is not a bill. It is a smoothing mechanism. Your supplier estimates how much energy you will use over the next twelve months, multiplies that by your tariff's unit rates, adds the daily standing charges, and divides the total by twelve. You overpay in summer, underpay in winter, and in theory the account balances out over a full year.

Three inputs drive that calculation, and a change in any of them changes your payment:

  • Your estimated annual consumption. Based on your meter reading history, or industry averages if the supplier has little data on you.
  • Your tariff rates. Unit rates and standing charges, which move every three months if you are on a price-capped variable tariff.
  • Your current account balance. A healthy credit cushion going into winter keeps payments stable. A debit balance, or less credit than the supplier's model expects for the time of year, pushes the payment up to recover the shortfall.

Because the figure is forward-looking, it can rise even when your past usage was modest. The supplier is pricing the year ahead, not the year behind. That is also why two neighbours with identical houses can pay different amounts: their reading history, balance, and tariff dates differ.

sections.h2: The seven reasons your payment went up
sections.html:
Almost every increase comes down to one of these. Your supplier's notification email or app usually says which, though often in vague wording.

1. The price cap changed

If you are on a standard variable tariff, Ofgem resets the cap on unit rates and standing charges every January, April, July and October. A cap rise feeds straight into your estimated annual cost, and the direct debit follows. Check which cap period the new payment is based on.

2. Your fixed deal ended

This is the single biggest jump most people ever see. When a fixed tariff expires and you do nothing, you roll onto the supplier's standard variable tariff at capped rates, which may be far above what you were paying. If this is you, read our guide to what happens when your fixed energy deal ends and compare the market promptly.

3. Your usage went up

A new baby, working from home, an electric car, a teenager discovering hour-long showers, a cold spring. If recent meter readings show higher consumption than last year, the supplier extrapolates that forward across a whole year, which amplifies the effect on the monthly figure.

4. Estimated readings caught up with reality

If you have not been submitting meter readings, the supplier has been guessing. When a real reading finally arrives and shows you used more than estimated, you get a catch-up bill and a higher ongoing payment in one hit. This is the most common cause of a shock increase for households without a smart meter.

5. You owe money on the account

If your account is in debit, the new payment includes a slice to repay it, typically spread over twelve months. The supplier should show the debt repayment element separately.

6. Your credit cushion is too thin for the season

Suppliers expect your balance to peak around October and bottom out around March. If a review in late summer finds less credit than their model wants before winter, they raise the payment even though you are technically in credit. This is legitimate but frequently overdone, and it is the increase most worth challenging.

7. The supplier reviewed its own assumptions

Suppliers periodically rerun their forecasting models, and reassessments can nudge everyone's payments. They are required to base your direct debit on the best available information, so a rise with no change in rates, usage or balance deserves a written explanation.

sections.h2: What a typical household should be paying right now
sections.html:
To judge whether your new payment is reasonable, you need a benchmark. Ofgem's typical dual-fuel household uses 2,700 kWh of electricity and 11,500 kWh of gas a year. At the current cap rates, that breaks down like this:

Cost elementCalculationAnnual cost
Electricity units2,700 kWh at 26.11p£705
Electricity standing charge365 days at 57.19p£209
Gas units11,500 kWh at 7.33p£843
Gas standing charge365 days at 29.04p£106
Total£1,863 (about £155 a month)

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, with standing charges of 57.19p and 29.04p per day, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026, GB direct-debit average. Checked June 2026.

Your own number will differ with your usage, but the method is the same. Find your annual kWh for each fuel on your latest statement or in your supplier's app, multiply by your unit rates, add 365 days of each standing charge, and divide by twelve. If your last twelve months of readings are accurate, that figure is what your direct debit should roughly be, plus a modest amount for any debt repayment. Our guide to understanding your energy bill shows where to find every one of these numbers on a statement.

Note how much of the total is standing charges: £315 a year before you use a single unit. That part of the bill does not respond to using less, which is why cutting usage sometimes reduces the bill less than people expect.

sections.h2: How to check whether the new amount is fair
sections.html:
Work through these in order. Most disputes resolve at step two or three.

  • Submit a meter reading today. If the increase rests on estimated readings, a real reading either confirms it or collapses it. Households with smart meters skip this step, since readings flow automatically.
  • Find the supplier's estimate of your annual usage. It appears on statements as "estimated annual consumption" or similar. Compare it with what you actually used over the last twelve months. If the estimate is inflated, say so and ask for it to be corrected.
  • Do the twelve-month calculation above with your real usage and your actual tariff rates. If the supplier's monthly figure is more than about 10 to 15 per cent above your calculation and you have no debt to repay, query it.
  • Check your balance trajectory. Being £200 in credit in September is normal and sensible. Being £400 in credit in March, after the winter draw-down, suggests you have been overpaying all year.
  • Check the rates you are being charged. If your fixed deal quietly ended, the unit rates on your statement will have jumped. That is your cue to compare deals: see our hub on switching suppliers for how to do it without risk.

Keep a note of the date and reading whenever you check. A short paper trail makes any later complaint much faster to resolve.

sections.h2: Your rights when you disagree with an increase
sections.html:
Suppliers do not have a free hand here. Ofgem rules require direct debits to be based on the best available information, and after a 2022 review of poor practice, suppliers must be able to justify the amount they charge. In practice that gives you several concrete levers:

  • Ask for a written justification. A simple "please explain how my new payment was calculated, showing the usage estimate and rates used" obliges them to show their working.
  • Ask for the payment to be set to a specific figure. If your own calculation supports a lower amount, propose it. Many suppliers let you adjust the payment within a band in the app without speaking to anyone.
  • Request a refund of surplus credit. You are entitled to ask for credit back at any time, and the supplier must refund it or give a sound reason not to. Asking just after winter, when your balance should be at its natural low, is the strongest moment.
  • Use the back-billing rule. If a catch-up charge covers energy used more than twelve months ago and the supplier was at fault for not billing you, they cannot charge you for the portion older than twelve months.
  • Escalate if needed. Raise a formal complaint with the supplier first. If it is not resolved within eight weeks, or they issue a deadlock letter, you can take it to the Energy Ombudsman free of charge, and its decisions bind the supplier.

One thing not to do: cancelling the direct debit at your bank in protest. You lose the direct debit pricing discount, the debt does not go away, and missed payments can lead to debt collection or a forced switch to prepayment. Negotiate the amount instead; suppliers adjust payments far more readily than most people assume. Scheme rules and complaint procedures change, so check the current guidance on the Ofgem website before relying on a specific rule.

A direct debit rise is an estimate, not a verdict. It moves for seven knowable reasons: cap changes, an expired fix, higher usage, corrected estimates, account debt, a thin winter cushion, or a supplier reassessment. Benchmark it against the maths: a typical home pays about £155 a month at the July to September 2026 cap, and your own figure is your annual kWh times your rates plus standing charges, divided by twelve. Submit a real meter reading, run that calculation, and if the supplier's number does not survive contact with your own, challenge it. They must justify the figure, you can have surplus credit refunded, and the Ombudsman sits behind it all if they dig in. And if the rise is simply because your cheap fix ended, the fix is not negotiating with your current supplier but shopping around.

faq.q: Can my supplier increase my direct debit without telling me?
faq.a: No. They must notify you before the new amount is collected, normally at least ten working days ahead under the Direct Debit Guarantee, and Ofgem rules require the amount to be based on the best available information about your usage and balance. If a higher payment was taken without notice, complain and ask for it to be corrected.

faq.q: Why has my direct debit gone up when I am in credit?
faq.a: Suppliers set payments to build credit through summer so it can drain through winter. If your balance is lower than their model expects for the time of year, they raise the payment even though you are in credit. It can be legitimate, but if you are still well in credit at the end of winter you have been overpaying and should ask for a reduction and a refund.

faq.q: Can I get my credit balance refunded?
faq.a: Yes. You can request a refund of credit at any time, and your supplier must pay it promptly or give a clear reason not to, such as winter being imminent. The best time to ask is spring, when your balance is at its seasonal low and anything left over is genuine surplus.

faq.q: Should I cancel my direct debit if I think the increase is wrong?
faq.a: No. Cancelling at the bank loses you the direct debit discount, leaves any debt in place, and can lead to debt collection or being moved to prepayment. Challenge the amount instead: ask for the calculation, propose a figure backed by your own usage, and escalate to the Energy Ombudsman if the supplier will not engage after eight weeks.

faq.q: How often should my supplier review my direct debit?
faq.a: At least once a year under Ofgem rules, and most suppliers review payments every six months or after each quarterly price cap change. You do not have to wait: you can ask for a review, or adjust the payment yourself in many suppliers' apps, whenever your usage or rates change.
